author: @praisebewibble
text:
Solar power is underutilized. Im sitting in a boat that uses solsr for boiling the kettle, a small tv, a small 220v compressor fridge, all the lights and i just cooked a steak on the induction hob. All only from 2  x 175 watt solar panels equipped with a mppt charger and a 2kva inverter , not forgetting of course the 150amp hour litium battery. Its a sunny day today but I can assure you that all summer long the fridge is working 24/7. 
Useless in the winter , mind you but for the summer months , brilliant
.

author: @wazza33racer
text: Its more sustainable to use solar thermal collectors to heat a high temperature liquid to drive a stirling engine.  The efficiency is as good or better than PV and doesnt need ridiculous toxic materials and could last a very long time.......the only limit would be the number of operating hours a stirling engine can last for.   Another bonus is that the hot liquid can be stored after sundown and continue to drive the stirling engine........a thermal battery. PV panels will have to improve, or they will become a massive,toxic recycling nightmare.   OR just build Thorium MSR (molten salt reactors).

author: @JSabh
text: The solution is simple yet takes work many are not willing to do. Three basic steps, 1: grow hemp ( it absorbs 1.4X the amount of CO2 that it expels when burned so in effect, it removes CO2 ) 2: Harvest and process ( dry it, grind it, and put it into a sealed vessel to heat it to 1200 F and gasify it ) 3: While gasifying, the reaction produces syngas and liquid petroleum ( run a standard generator while the reaction takes place, store the oil and use it when needed all the while removing CO2 from the atmosphere).. tada. Seriously, it is that simple. Use solar and wind to run the reaction if you want, or use the stored fuel oil and have a great day with no relying on anyone, just the rain and sun. The thing that stops people is the work, I gladly put the work in and it serves me very well.

author: @seeker1015
text:
I was born in Adelaide South Australia where we had, in the 50's, thousands of the original Swedish flat pack houses built. Ours was in the Eastern suburbs. 

Sadly, most are demolished now or have been trucked to new life's in the country. My sister lived in one until recently and her house was only just chomped up and sent to land fill a week ago to make way for two units on the one block. Probably with black roofs, the in thing apparently! The house I was born in is still standing, as is its neighbour because they have both been asbestos clad when I was several years old and removing asbestos is a headache and a huge cost. Mum didn't like the idea, cost and labour of repainting every decade so she it the bullet and had it covered in big sheets of the stuff.

Bizarrely, when they are moved, know nothing councils insist that they have more wall studs put into them before being approved. Never known one to blow down or apart in my 69 years, but that's councils.

Hopefully, while the re-studding is being done, insulation will be put into the walls. 
Sister found out from someone who had done a study on the history of them, that when they arrived at our local port and were unpacked, the workers threw away loads of wood wool, thinking it was "just packing" but it was Swedish insulation. Given SA's, at times, fierce Mediterranean climate and frosty winter , it would have been a boon when I was growing up.
The ceilings were self supporting and meant all the internal walls could be removed or shifted around without problems.

On solar thermal, I have an evacuated tube water heater, which gives loads of free hot water and is not affected by frost like flat plate heaters are.

If I had another, I could use it to provide skirting board heating.

author: @thefoamcollective1308
text:
We live in the Baltimore Washington DC metro area, and so are well acquainted with Levitt houses, with Bowie MD about halfway between these two cities.The main problem with the Levittowns were the white only policies that they enforced for most of the communities they built. Also they often sacrificed quality for quantity, and got into trouble for both practices in the 60s and 70s. Still their assembly process and the layout of the communities were groundbreaking for their time. As a point of interest, those same houses are selling for around $500,000 at this point.
Thanks for the history and your amazing channel!

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
In 1904' Manuel António Gomes, best known as Padre Himalaya, a Portuguese priest, scientist and inventor, won the St. Louis World's Fair Science and Technology prize over Edison's electric lights.  The device he won with was a mass of metal and mirror called the Pyrheliophor Solar Furnace. Among other things (acting as a telescope at night) in the daytime it was capable of reaching temperatures of 7000°F.  

Although a prise winner, no one  wanted to buy, dismantle, and transport after the Fair ended, and nothing much more came of it after that.

His designs are worth looking into, including a solar cooker that you can easily tilt away from the sun and access from under the mirror which might be adaptable to power a home solar Stirling engine/generator.

author: @Cryptic_Triptych
text:
Lest we forget. Every LED is also a PV. There should be absolutely no reason to plug in your phone to charge it. It can be wired to self-charging.

Secondly, the use of radioactive salts, like those used by T H Moray, to build a lead lined nested radioactive salt generator, is all you need. A portable shoebox sized (6 layered) device will generate 0.5 kw/h or 12,000 watts per 24 hours. Additionally, like Sir Siemens, Tesla and Schauberger, Moray built a Radiant Energy Tower which produced over 600 watts.

This can be easily built, set up and operated by anyone.

replies.author: @j.f.christ8421
replies.text:
Power from phone screens?  Nah...  Most are LCD anyway, with a LED backlight.  Sounds like you've never checked how much power you get from a LED, it's not much.  You should try it one day.

As for radioactive batteries, we already have those.  They even have a name: betavoltaics.  As the name suggests beta particles cause a current flow in PV junctions.

As for "operated by anyone", why haven't you done it?

replies.author: @Cryptic_Triptych
replies.text:
@J.F. Christ  sounds like you don't understand what amount "not much" is. You should definitely look into that. 

I'm sure you already know beta and alfa voltaic batteries are used for a low power applications over a long period. Which is exactly opposite of what we are discussing here 🤔 

I have done it, built a TREC, just like Hermann Plauson, Eric Dollard, Lawrence Rayburn and Ion Power Group.

Great burner account BTW. Are you from Virginia?

replies.author: @j.f.christ8421
replies.text:
@Cryptic_Triptych  You haven't built anything, otherwise you'd have mentioned it in your first post.

And you've never tested a LED, otherwise you'd not be suggesting that either.  LEDs generating power has been known forever, a good trick is to use them as light sensors (ordinary ones, not photodiodes).  Perhaps "sod all" is a better description of the power LEDs generate.

Cut the top off a transistor, shine light on it and you'll get some power too.  It's PV junctions all the way down, baby.  (BTW, we call those phototransistors).

And no, I'm not a septic.  Seems I'm not the only person to point out your rubbish, that doesn't surprise me.

And you forgot to mention Tesla.  Tsk tsk.

replies.text:

author: @davidprocter3578
text: Of course we had our own huge prefab estates here in the UK. Built to house bombed out civilians, they were built from precast concrete and asbestos panels. Much loved by many of their residents as they represented quite an upgrade in their living conditions and for the first time in their lives had a small garden. I remember well the demolition of these large estates back in the early to mid sixties, and how people moaned at the loss of their homes, many of whom were moved into soulless blocks of flats. Politics eh you got to love it. But other than their Crittall windows I don't recall  any other real solar. Quite a number of these post war prefabs survived up to the nineties  and have seen the odd one asbestos stripped  within the last twenty years. Does anyone know of a WW2 prefab still standing near them?

replies.author: @q.e.d.9112
replies.text:
Of course, and obviously. Even if the source of the energy costs nothing, the infrastructure has a capital cost and there are ongoing maintenance costs. Why would “they” make that available to you for free.

BTW. The use of an unspecified “they” as the “baddies” of your hypothesis should be avoided unless you’re happy for readers to assume you’re a full-time, conspiracy theorist.

author: @deltonlomatai2309
text: It is still going on in the US.  The compromise now is that you can have solar electric as long as your connected to the grid and can sell your excess power to the electric companies.  The US has a large Natural Gas infrastructure with gas being provided to a number of houses for heating and cooking.   I have to wonder if it would be better to have your own natural gas powered generator?  There is a problem if the gas stop flowing.  People will have problems anyway because the electric control and blowers  of gas furnaces run on electricity when the power stops the heating shuts down. In the big texas freeze, power outage did shut down gas distribution leading to freezing and busted water pipes.   Some people who had gasoline backup generators or maybe home solar got along fairly well.  How effective is solar in winter? It would be interesting to watch how that all occurred.  I believe it was because the pumping station were on grid power and the gas distributers did not have a backup power system when the electricity went out.  Leading to the big Texas freeze.

author: @vivviedarling9336
text: I really enjoy your content, I would love to reduce my reliance on heating oil. I am almost on empty, and will cost over £1000 to refill my tank, wholesale price is rock bottom but cost to me as consumer is as high as it was last December. I'm only one person and I cannot keep paying these prices. I am actively seeking Earth-friendly alternatives.

replies.author: @dav1dbone
replies.text: Normally these are shared costs between a group of neighbours, sounds like you are paying a premium as it's just a single household. Did you consider a wood burner?

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: Is there any way you could collect and filter cooking oil from nearby restaurants? That should cost you next to nothing. It works with diesel cars so I'm assuming it would work just as well in a furnace.

author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
text:
And with what Robert told in the video and seeing that planned Obsolescence is the highest form of modern innovation in capitalism...

Happy Capitalism Everyone!

It never was about delivering the best product or service for the consumer, but about how to take away as much of the Workers hard earned money as possible - with the lowest effort possible.

This is being done by the accumulation of wealth and monopolizing whole industries.
As an example, we may have hundreds of different flavours of cereal...
But 80% of all Brands are from just 3 Companies, which divided the markets equal between each other and can outcompete everyone trying to get a bigger share of the market.
And that is just one market...

Mobiles, cars, water, energy, whatever -you name it- and you'll see that the total number of companies dwindled down to a single digit few in each sector in the last few years.

And as more and more companies start to switch to you not owning anything after buying, but renting everything and already build in features are only activated IF you pay a monthly Fee, we will be turned from "we-the-free-people" to debt-slaves in a worldwide company store
Even as we see possibilities of how to generate a fair bit yourself here, the powers that are will, rather sooner then later, fight every alternative source of energy not under a capitalists control, mostly with a hundred logical laws and regulations, naturally just for the safety of the consumers…

Happy Capitalism Everyone!
Happy Capitalism Everyone indeed…

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
Robert, I'd like to point out that, post WW Deuce, 30 year mortgages were the exception. Most Long-Term mortgages were 20 to 25 years, and only done for the most egregiously expensive houses.
Most properties like the Levitt house would have had a 3 to 5 year note, tops.

replies.author: @AlienLivesMatter
replies.text: Nice detail

replies.author: @turkeytrac1
replies.text: Maybe in the states but my WW2 veteran grandparents were on a 25 yr mortgage here in Canada. With no need to renegotiate every 4-5 yrs.

replies.author: @alspezial2747
replies.text: Here in germany, you pay around 80 cents per kw/h, but when you have solar on your roof and sell it back, you only get 7 cents per kw/h

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: @alspezial2747 Same here in Canada we get the production value as credit and not the other parts like distribution and line maintenance.

replies.author: @alspezial2747
replies.text: @OffGrid Wanabe  actually makes quite a lot more sense now that you say it

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: @alspezial2747 The real savings is finding how to use or save all the solar that you can make. Instead of subsidizing the utility company with cheap power, then you get the full 80cents a kwh or if used in a vehicle the price of gasoline.
